lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: What, ho! my lord!

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: Who knocks at the door?

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: A messenger from the Lord Stanley.

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: What is’t o’clock?

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: Upon the stroke of four.

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: Cannot thy master sleep these tedious nights?

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: So it should seem by that I have to say.

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: First, he commends him to your noble lordship.

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: And then?

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: And then he sends you word

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: He dreamt to-night the boar had razed his helm:

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: Besides, he says there are two councils held;

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: And that may be determined at the one

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: which may make you and him to rue at the other.

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: Therefore he sends to know your lordship’s pleasure,

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: If presently you will take horse with him,

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: And with all speed post with him toward the north,

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: To shun the danger that his soul divines.

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: Go, fellow, go, return unto thy lord;

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: Bid him not fear the separated councils:

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: His honour and myself are at the one,

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: And at the other is my servant Catesby;

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: Where nothing can proceed that toucheth us

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: Whereof I shall not have intelligence.

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: Tell him his fears are shallow, wanting instance:

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: And for his dreams, I wonder he is so fond

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: To trust the mockery of unquiet slumbers:

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: To fly the boar before the boar pursues,

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: Were to incense the boar to follow us

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: And make pursuit where he did mean no chase.

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: Go, bid thy master rise and come to me;

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: And we will both together to the Tower,

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: Where, he shall see, the boar will use us kindly.

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: My gracious lord, I’ll tell him what you say.

lines.speaker: CATESBY
lines.text: Many good morrows to my noble lord!

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: Good morrow, Catesby; you are early stirring:

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: What news, what news, in this our tottering state?

lines.speaker: CATESBY
lines.text: It is a reeling world, indeed, my lord;

lines.speaker: CATESBY
lines.text: And I believe ’twill never stand upright

lines.speaker: CATESBY
lines.text: Till Richard wear the garland of the realm.

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: How! wear the garland! dost thou mean the crown?

lines.speaker: CATESBY
lines.text: Ay, my good lord.

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: I’ll have this crown of mine cut from my shoulders

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: Ere I will see the crown so foul misplaced.

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: But canst thou guess that he doth aim at it?

lines.speaker: CATESBY
lines.text: Ay, on my life; and hopes to find you forward

lines.speaker: CATESBY
lines.text: Upon his party for the gain thereof:

lines.speaker: CATESBY
lines.text: And thereupon he sends you this good news,

lines.speaker: CATESBY
lines.text: That this same very day your enemies,

lines.speaker: CATESBY
lines.text: The kindred of the queen, must die at Pomfret.

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: Indeed, I am no mourner for that news,

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: Because they have been still mine enemies:

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: But, that I’ll give my voice on Richard’s side,

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: To bar my master’s heirs in true descent,

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: God knows I will not do it, to the death.

lines.speaker: CATESBY
lines.text: God keep your lordship in that gracious mind!

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: But I shall laugh at this a twelve-month hence,

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: That they who brought me in my master’s hate,

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: I live to look upon their tragedy.

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: I tell thee, Catesby—

lines.speaker: CATESBY
lines.text: What, my lord?

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: Ere a fortnight make me elder,

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: I’ll send some packing that yet think not on it.

lines.speaker: CATESBY
lines.text: ’Tis a vile thing to die, my gracious lord,

lines.speaker: CATESBY
lines.text: When men are unprepared and look not for it.

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: O monstrous, monstrous! and so falls it out

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: With Rivers, Vaughan, Grey: and so ’twill do

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: With some men else, who think themselves as safe

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: As thou and I; who, as thou know’st, are dear

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: To princely Richard and to Buckingham.

lines.speaker: CATESBY
lines.text: The princes both make high account of you;

lines.speaker: CATESBY
lines.text: For they account his head upon the bridge.

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: I know they do; and I have well deserved it.

lines.speaker: STANLEY
lines.text: My lord, good morrow; good morrow, Catesby:

lines.speaker: STANLEY
lines.text: You may jest on, but, by the holy rood,

lines.speaker: STANLEY
lines.text: I do not like these several councils, I.

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: My lord,

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: I hold my life as dear as you do yours;

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: And never in my life, I do protest,

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: Was it more precious to me than ’tis now:

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: Think you, but that I know our state secure,

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: I would be so triumphant as I am?

lines.speaker: STANLEY
lines.text: The lords at Pomfret, when they rode from London,

lines.speaker: STANLEY
lines.text: Were jocund, and supposed their state was sure,

lines.speaker: STANLEY
lines.text: And they indeed had no cause to mistrust;

lines.speaker: STANLEY
lines.text: But yet, you see how soon the day o’ercast.

lines.speaker: STANLEY
lines.text: This sudden stab of rancour I misdoubt:

lines.speaker: STANLEY
lines.text: Pray God, I say, I prove a needless coward!

lines.speaker: STANLEY
lines.text: What, shall we toward the Tower? the day is spent.

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: Come, come, have with you. Wot you what, my lord?

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: To-day the lords you talk of are beheaded.

lines.speaker: STANLEY
lines.text: They, for their truth, might better wear their heads

lines.speaker: STANLEY
lines.text: Than some that have accused them wear their hats.

lines.speaker: STANLEY
lines.text: But come, my lord, let us away.

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: Go on before; I’ll talk with this good fellow.

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: How now, sirrah! how goes the world with thee?

lines.speaker: PURSUIVANT
lines.text: The better that your lordship please to ask.

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: I tell thee, man, ’tis better with me now

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: Than when I met thee last where now we meet:

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: Then was I going prisoner to the Tower,

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: By the suggestion of the queen’s allies;

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: But now, I tell thee—keep it to thyself—

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: This day those enemies are put to death,

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: And I in better state than e’er I was.

lines.speaker: PURSUIVANT
lines.text: God hold it, to your honour’s good content!

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: Gramercy, fellow: there, drink that for me.

lines.speaker: PURSUIVANT
lines.text: God save your lordship!

lines.speaker: PRIEST
lines.text: Well met, my lord; I am glad to see your honour.

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: I thank thee, good Sir John, with all my heart.

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: I am in your debt for your last exercise;

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: Come the next Sabbath, and I will content you. He whispers in his ear.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: What, talking with a priest, lord chamberlain?

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Your friends at Pomfret, they do need the priest;

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Your honour hath no shriving work in hand.

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: Good faith, and when I met this holy man,

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: Those men you talk of came into my mind.

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: What, go you toward the Tower?

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: I do, my lord; but long I shall not stay:

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: I shall return before your lordship thence.

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: ’Tis like enough, for I stay dinner there.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: And supper too, although thou know’st it not.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Come, will you go?

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: I’ll wait upon your lordship.

stage_directions.text: Come on, come on; where is your boar-spear, man? Fear you the boar, and go so unprovided?

glosses.definition: the boar had torn off his helmet — Stanley's dream; the boar is Richard's heraldic badge, so the dream is a warning of decapitation
regret at the other (council)
superficial, without evidence
foolish

glosses.phrase: fly the boar before the boar pursues
glosses.definition: flee the boar before it hunts you — to run is to invite pursuit; Hastings' fatal rationalization
unsteady political condition
dizzy, drunken-staggering world
the crown (garland = circular ornament for the head)
eager on his side

glosses.phrase: bar my master’s heirs in true descent
glosses.definition: block the legitimate succession of my king's heirs — Hastings' refusal point, his death-warrant
in theological terms: unshriven, not absolved — a grave spiritual danger
dear (beloved) to princely Richard — dramatic irony: Hastings believes he is
his head mounted on London Bridge, the traditional display for traitors — Catesby's grim pun (Hastings thinks it means 'high regard')
holy cross; common oath
cheerful, merry
unexpected stroke of malice
do you know what; listen
junior heraldic officer; low-ranking court attendant
many thanks (from French grand merci)
courtesy title for a priest — 'Sir' prefixed to the first name of a clergyman
last sermon (an 'exercise' was a homiletic or devotional lesson)
work of confession; spiritual preparation for death — Buckingham's black joke
and the supper of death — Buckingham's aside menaces what Hastings thinks is a joke

annotations.title: Stanley's boar-dream as political reading
annotations.body: Stanley sends his messenger to Hastings at four in the morning with a reading of a dream: "the boar had razed his helm." The boar is Richard's heraldic badge (the white boar of Gloucester), and the image of a boar removing a helmet — i.e., exposing the head — is a direct warning of decapitation. Stanley asks Hastings to flee north with him. Hastings dismisses the dream with the characteristic Elizabethan scepticism toward oneiromancy ("I wonder he is so fond / To trust the mockery of unquiet slumbers") and adds the fatal second argument: to run from the boar is to invite pursuit. The logic is sound in general, wrong in this case. Hastings has misread what kind of play he is in. Shakespeare gives the reader the epistemological joke: dream-reading and political intuition converge in this play, and dismissing either costs Hastings his head before evening.
sources: Keith Thomas, Religion and the Decline of Magic (1971)

annotations.title: Hastings' one line he will not cross
annotations.body: Catesby probes Hastings on whether he will support Richard's claim to the crown, and Hastings draws the line exactly where we expect him to: "I'll have this crown of mine cut from my shoulders / Ere I will see the crown so foul misplaced." The line is principled and self-aware: Hastings knows what he is saying, knows he is marking himself as obstacle, and says it anyway. The scene thus portrays a figure who is not simply a Richard-type careerist but a courtier with a limit. His subsequent miscalculation is not a failure of principle but of recognition: having drawn the line, he thinks he remains safe because the queen's kindred (his old enemies) are being executed at Pomfret that same day. He cannot hold two political logics at once — Richard's killing of the queen's men and Richard's coming killing of him — and the scene lets the audience see the cognitive trap.
sources: A. P. Rossiter, Angel with Horns (1961)
Emrys Jones, Scenic Form in Shakespeare (1971)

annotations.title: The priest, the pursuivant, and Buckingham's joke
annotations.body: The scene closes with Hastings' trio of encounters: a pursuivant (junior court officer), a priest ("Sir John"), and Buckingham, each greeted with increasing cheer. Hastings is, at this moment, the most confident man in England. Buckingham's aside — "Your honour hath no shriving work in hand" — is a black joke turning on the Catholic sacrament of confession ("shriving"). Hastings dismisses the priest as unneeded; Buckingham silently marks the irony that Hastings will need a priest urgently within hours. The scene uses minor functionaries — pursuivant, priest — as choric witnesses to Hastings' fatal unknowing. Shakespeare learned this technique from Christopher Marlowe (who used small characters to frame large falls in Edward II), but Richard III refines it into the play's signature rhythm.
sources: Jonathan Bate, The Genius of Shakespeare (1997)

trials.prompt: What does Stanley's dream about "the boar" and "his helm" warn of?
options: Decapitation: the boar is Richard's heraldic badge, and a boar removing a helmet pictures a head exposed

trials.prompt: Why does Hastings refuse to flee north with Stanley?
options: He is loyal to the crown and will not abandon his post
He distrusts dream-reading generally, and reasons that fleeing the boar would invite pursuit — sound logic misapplied to this specific situation
He has been promised lands if he remains
He is physically unable to travel

trials.prompt: When Hastings tells Catesby "I'll have this crown of mine cut from my shoulders / Ere I will see the crown so foul misplaced," what is the dramatic function of the line?
options: A principled refusal — Hastings draws the one line he will not cross, and the play immediately makes that line his death-warrant
A promise to fight Richard in single combat
A legal objection to Buckingham's proposal

trials.prompt: Buckingham's aside to Hastings — "Your honour hath no shriving work in hand" — puns on what?
options: The medieval tradition of trial by combat
The Catholic sacrament of confession ("shriving") — Hastings will in fact need a priest urgently within hours
The legal requirement for witnesses at an execution
A specific guild's ceremony of initiation
